1.1.section: 1. Data description and collection or re-use of existing data
1.1.question: How will new data be collected or produced and/or how will existing data be re-used?
1.1.content:
The project will combine the re-use of existing scientific data with the generation of new theoretical and
computational research data. Existing data will include publicly available molecular machine-learning benchmark
datasets, molecular descriptor datasets, thermophysical datasets, and data reported in the scientific literature.
These datasets will be used for empirical validation of the proposed framework.
New data will be generated through theoretical derivations, computational experiments, admissible realization
constructions, localization analyses, representation transformations, and machine-learning validation studies.
Generated data will include comparison structures, localization profiles, similarity matrices, model outputs, and
benchmarking results.
Data provenance, preprocessing procedures, software versions, and analytical workflows will be documented to
ensure reproducibility, traceability, and data quality.

1.2.section: 1. Data description and collection or re-use of existing data
1.2.question: What data (for example the types, formats, and volumes) will be collected or produced?
1.2.content:
The project will generate and re-use primarily digital research data. Re-used data will include molecular machine-
learning benchmark datasets, molecular descriptor datasets, thermophysical datasets, and data extracted from
scientific publications. Generated data will include admissible comparison structures, realization-space instances,
localization profiles, similarity matrices, representation mappings, machine-learning outputs, benchmarking results,
and statistical summaries.
Data formats will include CSV, TXT, JSON, PDF, PNG, and Python source files (.py), together with documentation files
(README). Most datasets will be tabular and machine-readable.
The expected volume of generated data is moderate, estimated at several gigabytes over the entire project. No
personal, medical, or sensitive data will be collected or processed.
Documentation and data quality

Documentation and data quality

2.1.question: What metadata and documentation (for example methodology or data collection and way of organising data) will accompany data?
2.1.content:
Each dataset will be accompanied by metadata describing its title, source, creator, creation date, version, file
format, and intended use. For re-used datasets, metadata will include original sources, citations, access dates, and
preprocessing procedures. For generated datasets, metadata will document the methodology used to produce the
data, software versions, parameter settings, and computational workflows.
Data will be organised using a structured folder hierarchy separating raw data, processed data, code, results, figures,
and documentation. Standard file naming conventions and version control will be used throughout the project.
Documentation will include README files describing dataset contents, variable definitions, units of measurement
(where applicable), data-processing steps, and relationships between files. Computational analyses will be
documented through annotated source code, scripts, and workflow descriptions to ensure reproducibility and
traceability.
Metadata and documentation will be maintained in machine-readable and human-readable formats and updated
throughout the project lifecycle.

Metadata and documentation will be maintained in machine-readable and human-readable formats and updated
throughout the project lifecycle.

2.2.question: What data quality control measures will be used?
2.2.content:
Data quality will be ensured through verification of data sources, documentation of data provenance, and
reproducible computational workflows. Re-used datasets will be checked for completeness, consistency, duplicate
records, formatting errors, and compatibility with project requirements.
Generated data will be validated through automated consistency checks, independent verification of computational
outputs, and systematic review of code and analytical procedures. All data transformations, preprocessing steps,
and parameter settings will be documented and version-controlled.
Quality assurance will also include testing the reproducibility of key results, maintaining audit trails for data
processing, and cross-checking selected analyses by project team members. These measures will minimize errors,
improve transparency, and ensure the reliability of research outputs.
Storage and backup during the research processHedayatollah Haddadi, Gdańsk University of Technology 680977

3.1.section: 3. Storage and backup during the research process
3.1.question: How will data and metadata be stored and backed up during the research process?
3.1.content:
Data and metadata will be stored on secure institutional computer systems and backed up regularly using
university-supported storage infrastructure. Working copies of data, source code, and documentation will be
maintained in version-controlled repositories with restricted write access for authorised project members.
The project will use a structured directory system separating raw data, processed data, code, results, and
documentation. Metadata, README files, and workflow descriptions will be stored together with the corresponding
datasets.
Backups will be performed regularly and maintained in multiple locations to minimise the risk of data loss. In the
event of hardware failure or accidental deletion, data will be recoverable from backup copies. Data integrity and
accessibility will be monitored throughout the project.

3.2.section: 3. Storage and backup during the research process
3.2.question: How will data security and protection of sensitive data be taken care of during the research?
3.2.content:
available scientific datasets, published literature, and computationally generated research data.
Data security will be ensured through storage on secure institutional systems with controlled access for authorised
project members. Source code, datasets, and documentation will be maintained in version-controlled repositories
and protected by institutional authentication mechanisms.
Regular backups will be performed to prevent data loss. Access rights will be managed according to project
responsibilities, and all data transfers will use secure institutional infrastructure. These measures will ensure the
confidentiality, integrity, availability, and long-term preservation of project data.
Legal requirements, codes of conduct

Legal requirements, codes of conduct

4.1.question: If personal data are processed, how will compliance with legislation on personal data and on data security be ensured?
4.1.content:
ensured?
The project is not expected to involve the collection or processing of personal data. Research activities will be based
on publicly available scientific datasets, published literature, and computationally generated research data.
Should any personal data be processed during the project, all applicable legal requirements, including the General
Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and institutional data protection policies, will be followed. Access to such data
would be restricted to authorised personnel, appropriate technical and organisational safeguards would be
implemented, and any required anonymisation or pseudonymisation procedures would be applied.

4.2.question: How will other legal issues, such as intellectual property rights and ownership, be managed? What legislation is applicable?
4.2.content:
Union legislation, the regulations of Gdańsk University of Technology, and the policies of the National Science
Centre. Research data, software, documentation, and other project outputs generated by the project team will be
owned and managed by the host institution in accordance with institutional regulations.
The project will primarily use publicly available datasets, scientific publications, and open-source software. Third-
party data and software will be used in compliance with their respective licences, terms of use, and copyright
requirements. Proper citation and attribution will be provided for all external resources.
Research outputs will be disseminated through scientific publications, software repositories, and research data
repositories whenever appropriate.
Data sharing and long-term preservation

Data sharing and long-term preservation

5.1.question: How and when will data be shared? Are there possible restrictions to data sharing or embargo reasons?
5.1.content:
Research data, software, and supporting documentation will be shared as openly as possible and as closed as
necessary, in accordance with FAIR principles and institutional policies. Data underlying scientific publications will
be made available no later than the publication of the corresponding research results through appropriate
repositories or project repositories.
The project is not expected to generate sensitive or confidential data. Therefore, no major restrictions on data
sharing are anticipated. Temporary embargoes may be applied when necessary to allow publication of research
results, preparation of software releases, or completion of ongoing analyses. Any such embargoes will be limited in
duration and justified by legitimate scientific considerations.
Metadata and documentation will remain openly accessible whenever possible.Hedayatollah Haddadi, Gdańsk University of Technology 680977

5.2.question: How will data for preservation be selected, and where will data be preserved long-term (for example a data repository or archive)?
5.2.content:
Data selected for long-term preservation will include datasets supporting published results, computational
workflows, source code, documentation, metadata, and other materials necessary to reproduce the research
findings. Temporary files, intermediate outputs, and redundant copies that do not contribute to reproducibility will
not be preserved.
Long-term preservation will be ensured through trusted institutional or public research repositories supporting FAIR
principles. Depending on the nature of the data, repositories such as Zenodo, institutional repositories, or discipline-
appropriate repositories will be used. Persistent identifiers (e.g. DOI) will be assigned whenever available.
Data and associated documentation will be retained for at least 10 years after project completion, in accordance
with good scientific practice and institutional policies.

5.3.question: What methods or software tools will be needed to access and use the data?
5.3.content:
The project will primarily use open and widely adopted software tools. Data will be accessible through standard
formats such as CSV, TXT, JSON, PDF, PNG, and Python source files. Access, processing, and analysis will require
common scientific computing tools, including Python and its scientific libraries.
Documentation, metadata, and README files will accompany all preserved datasets to facilitate interpretation and
reuse. No proprietary or specialised hardware is required to access the data. Wherever possible, open formats and
open-source software will be used to maximise long-term accessibility, interoperability, and reproducibility of the
research outputs.

5.4.question: How will the application of a unique and persistent identifier (such as a Digital Object Identifier (DOI)) to each data set be ensured?
5.4.content:
set be ensured?
Datasets selected for public sharing and long-term preservation will be deposited in repositories that provide
persistent identifiers, such as Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs). Repositories such as Zenodo, institutional
repositories, or other appropriate research data repositories will be used depending on the nature of the data.
Each deposited dataset will be accompanied by metadata, documentation, and version information to ensure
discoverability, citation, and reuse. Persistent identifiers assigned by the repository will be referenced in
publications, project reports, and related research outputs.
Where multiple versions of a dataset exist, versioning information will be maintained to ensure traceability and
reproducibility of the research.
Data management responsibilities and resources

Data management responsibilities and resources

6.1.question: Who (for example role, position, and institution) will be responsible for data management (i.e. the data steward)?
6.1.content:
The Principal Investigator, Hedayatollah Haddadi (Associate Professor, Gdańsk University of Technology), will be
responsible for data management and implementation of the Data Management Plan. Responsibilities will include
oversight of data collection, documentation, quality control, storage, backup procedures, data sharing, and long-
term preservation.
Project team members will follow established data-management procedures and contribute to maintaining data
quality, documentation, and reproducibility. The Data Management Plan will be reviewed periodically during the
project and updated when necessary to ensure compliance with institutional policies, FAIR principles, and National
Science Centre requirements.
What resources (for example financial and time) will be dedicated to data management and ensuring the data will be
FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Re-usable)?
Data management activities will be integrated into the normal research workflow of the project. Resources will
primarily consist of researcher time devoted to data documentation, metadata preparation, quality control, version
management, repository deposition, and preparation of materials for sharing and long-term preservation.
The project will use existing institutional computing and storage infrastructure and open-source software tools.
Public repositories supporting FAIR principles (e.g. Zenodo or institutional repositories) will be used for long-term
preservation and dissemination of selected datasets, software, and documentation. No substantial additional
financial resources are anticipated beyond standard project operating costs.
These measures will ensure that project data remain Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR).Hedayatollah Haddadi, Gdańsk University of Technology 680977
